files.SKILL.md:
# Code Built To Survive Its Own Run

You are working on code that real callers will use after you stop watching.
Build for that, not just for what is visibly checked in this turn.

## Read in this order before editing

1. The environment guide the workspace publishes — the contract for
   endpoints, services, and constraints. Treat it as authoritative; do
   not re-derive what it states.
2. The current instruction, verbatim. Do not paraphrase or expand it.
3. Whatever record the environment retains from earlier work in this
   session. Outcomes recorded there outrank both stored notes and the
   current source. If a stored note defends an approach the recorded
   outcomes contradict, the note is wrong and gets replaced this turn.
4. Anything in the workspace's notes area from earlier passes. They are
   part of the input, not background.
5. The validation surface the environment exposes. Read each check
   through, names included — file, function, and test names carry the
   author's intent and tell you which properties must hold.
6. Source code last. By the time you open it you should already know
   what it has to do.

If you cannot point to which of the above justifies the move you are
about to make, you are not ready to move.

## Properties any non-trivial change must hold

Answer each question in your head before declaring the work finished.

**Will it stay healthy under a long run?** Imagine the process up for a
month. Anything that grows without bound — a list, a dictionary keyed by
caller, retained handles, accumulating timestamps — is a defect even if
nothing visible fails today. Bound it at write time, drop entries when
their reason for existing is gone.

**Is it safe with several callers at once?** Trace the function under
two simultaneous entries hitting the same branch. If the result can flip
depending on which one sees the state first, then reading the state,
deciding, and committing the resulting write all need to sit inside one
small guarded region.

**Does duration arithmetic survive clock adjustments?** Wall clocks can
step back, freeze, or skip forward. For elapsed-time reasoning, lean on
a monotonic source — never the current time of day.

**Do the named edges behave?** Walk each parameter at zero, at one, at
the empty case, at the very large case, and at the negative case where
the type allows. Edges must either be correct or break audibly.

**Are the surrounding invariants still intact?** Repairing one property
while quietly weakening a neighbour is not a step forward. Design the
final shape once so it satisfies every property together, then re-check
what the patch touched.

## Operating the workspace

Stage by exact path; blanket adds drag caches and editor temporaries
into the diff. Configure the version-control identity once before the
first commit — missing identity blocks commits silently. Branch off the
most recent relevant task branch rather than the default branch, so
already-shipped work is not silently reverted.

Multi-line file writes go through a small helper that holds the payload
in a triple-quoted Python string and writes it atomically. Shell
heredocs and `echo` corrupt backticks, dollar signs, and triple-quoted
content; that is the failure mode that turns a careful written artifact
into an unreadable one on disk.

## How you actually work the turn

**Orient, then translate.** The reads above tell you what "correct"
means; each property implies something specific for this contract. If
you cannot name the implication, you have not read enough.

**Pick a shape that satisfies every property at once** — not just the
shape that clears what is visibly tested.

**Produce a real artifact early.** After the minimum useful reading,
write the file, run the change, commit the diff. A turn that ends in
careful notes and no committed change did not finish. Commit early-mid,
never on the last available step — finalisation needs room.

**Verify what you claim.** Run the validation the environment provides
and read the output exactly; do not infer from "the call returned
without error". For your own writes, re-read the file or re-query the
resource afterwards — one extra look catches silent failures no
pre-flight check can.

## Persistent notes

Treat the workspace's notes area as working memory across runs, not a
journal. One short paragraph per entry is enough. Verified observations
only — broken assumptions, tooling quirks, recurring shapes worth
re-using. Write the moment a surprise lands; deferred = lost.

A note that no longer reflects the current environment misleads the
next run. Supersede or remove the stale entry the same turn you spot
it. Promote repeated observations into rules; retire frozen specifics
to history. Keep session-only details out of files meant to outlive the
session.

## Recovery from uncertainty

When blocked, do four things in order: name the missing fact, retrieve
it from the environment, choose the narrowest safe action that respects
what you do know, then verify the result. If retrieval is impossible,
state the gap directly instead of guessing.

## Finish cleanly

Before declaring done, re-read the instruction, confirm the requested
artifact exists in the requested location, and confirm the validation
passed on its own terms.
